message:
perf(func): borrow text/blob bytes in HEX() instead of cloning

`HEX(...)` was unconditionally allocating a fresh `Vec<u8>` for the
input bytes — cloning blob payloads via `b.to_vec()` and roundtripping
text args through `to_text().into_bytes()`. For the common case where
the SQL value is already a `Text` or `Blob`, the bytes are reachable
directly and the clone was pure waste on every call.

Switch to a `Cow<'_, [u8]>`:

- `SqliteValue::Blob(b)` → `Cow::Borrowed(b.as_ref())`. The `Arc<[u8]>`
  payload exposes its bytes without cloning the buffer, so the hex
  loop reads straight from the original allocation.
- `SqliteValue::Text(text)` → `Cow::Borrowed(text.as_bytes_direct())`.
  `SmallText::as_bytes_direct` already returns the UTF-8 byte view
  used by other builtins; HEX can reuse it without going through the
  `to_text()` String detour.
- All other variants → `Cow::Owned(other.to_text().into_bytes())`.
  Numeric/null formatting still has to allocate the textual form; we
  preserve the prior behavior for those cases.

The hex-encode loop changes from `for b in &bytes` to
`for b in bytes.as_ref()` to handle either Cow arm uniformly. The
`String::with_capacity(bytes.len() * 2)` pre-sizing is unchanged, so
the output buffer is still a single allocation.

Tests:

- New `perf_hex_text_blob_args` (gated `#[ignore = "perf-only benchmark"]`)
  drives `HexFunc::invoke` 100,000× with both a 24-byte text arg and a
  24-byte blob arg, repeats 5× and reports the best run for each.
  Black-boxed via `std::hint::black_box` to keep the optimizer honest.
  Local runs show the text path drops from a per-call alloc to
  zero on hot, and the blob path from a per-call clone of the Arc
  payload to a borrow.

`Cow` is already imported at the top of the file (`use std::borrow::Cow`)
from prior work, so no new imports needed.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
